data.title: Daily Voice: Budget support likely for realty; small caps to rebound, says Pankaj Pandey
data.description:
Pankaj Pandey is the Head of Retail Research at ICICI Direct
  • Real estate sector may receive maximum support in Union Budget
  • Real estate sector has the ability to create millions of jobs directly and indirectly
  • Small caps likely to make a comeback this year
Did our AI summary help?

"Real estate sector may receive the most support owing to its 7-8% GDP contribution, correlation with other allied industries (such as cement, steel, building materials), and being the second largest employer after agriculture," said Pankaj Pandey, the Head of Retail Research at ICICI Direct, in an interview with Moneycontrol.

According to him, affordable housing, which has been under stress, may get relief measures such as expanding the definition of affordable housing in terms of value and unit sizes.

He expects Small Caps to make a comeback this year. "With the small-cap index down ~6% in CY25 and ~13% from its all-time high, odds (86% probability) are in favour of small caps resuming the up move and delivering healthy double-digit returns in CY26," he said.

Which sectors do you expect to receive the most support from the government in the Union Budget 2026?

Real estate sector may receive the most support owing to its 7-8% GDP contribution, correlation with other allied industries (such as cement, steel, building materials), and being the second largest employer after agriculture. Affordable housing, which has been under stress, may get relief measures such as expanding the definition of affordable housing in terms of value and unit sizes.

Other measures such as increasing home loan interest deduction limit, increasing credit access for developers, incentives for first-time buyers, single window clearances, grant of infrastructure status, incentivising GCCs, promoting rental housing, etc.

Do you expect the government to announce concrete measures in the Budget to boost employment, especially as many experts have flagged this as a key risk?

Real estate’s ripple effect on more than 200 other allied industries, such as cement, steel, building materials, services, logistics, etc., has the ability to create millions of jobs directly and indirectly. Thus, measures here could boost employment generation objectives.

Similarly, the Indian Textile sector, employing ~45 million people, is currently reeling under the pressure of high tariffs imposed by the US on Indian textile exports. Any measures could support textile exports, including relief schemes/ tax concessions for setting up new units and permanent removal of 11% cotton import duty to reduce cost pressure, thereby aiding employment objectives.

Do you see the market emerging from the consolidation phase of the past year, or do you expect consolidation to continue due to global factors?

Given the fluid situation across trade tariffs and geopolitical tensions, we do expect the first quarter to witness a consolidation unless these issues witness some stabilisation. However, we expect investors to focus on corporate earnings and macro-economic growth as we move further in CY26.

Do you continue to favour private sector banks over PSU banks, or are you taking a strong, broad-based view on the entire banking sector?

The banking sector is benefiting from benign asset quality trends, structurally lower credit cost, and greater balance-sheet resilience following a multi-year clean-up. Importantly, increasing focus on the RAM (Retail, Agriculture, MSME) segment and improved loan granularity are reducing earnings volatility. Combined with comfortable capital positions and disciplined growth, this shift is supporting more sustainable RoA.

Thus, we continue to remain instrumental in the banking sector, with a slight tilt towards PSU banks and mid-cap private banks.

Are you bullish on the auto and metal sectors for the current year?

Auto and Metals have been the top-performing sectors with Nifty Auto up 21% and Nifty Metals up 36% over the last 1 year. Most of the investable stocks have moved up; hence, the sector is not a blanket buy anymore. However, there are select pockets which we continue to like, such as the Passenger Vehicle domain in autos (backed by longevity of growth) and ferrous players in the Metals (earnings supported by imposition of safeguard duty).

Do you expect a pickup in momentum in mid- and small-cap stocks, given their underperformance relative to benchmark indices?

Yes, we expect Small Caps to make a comeback this year. Interestingly, analysing the data for the last 2 decades, it has been observed that there exists a low probability (14%) of small caps correcting in two consecutive years. Therefore, with small cap index down ~6% in CY25 and ~13% from its all-time high, odds (86% probability) are in favour of small caps resuming the up move and delivering healthy double digit returns in CY26E.

Do you see the possibility of a continuation of the federal funds rate cut cycle in the January meeting?

The Federal delivered its third consecutive 25-basis-point rate cut in December, but the probability of another rate cut in January seems quite low, considering their projection of just 1 rate cut in CY26. The Fed has lifted its expectations of growth for 2026, which, along with the uncertainty regarding the inflation trajectory, will make it difficult for them to cut rates in the coming meeting.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by experts on Moneycontrol are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Fund managers flag short-term pain but remain confident on Indian equities despite market rout
data.description:
Global developments have played a central role in shaping sentiment. Heightened geopolitical tensions, uncertainty around global trade and renewed focus on tariffs dominated investor discussions, amidst the ongoing World Economic Forum in Davos. Comments around trade protectionism, including continued rhetoric from US President Donald Trump on imposing higher tariffs, threats on Greenland etc., added to concerns about the global growth outlook and cross-border trade flows.
  • Indian markets face short-term volatility due to global risks and weak earnings
  • Foreign investors continue selling, hitting mid- and small-cap stocks hardest
  • Fund managers see selective opportunities as valuations become attractive
Did our AI summary help?

Indian equity markets could face continued volatility in the short-term after a sharp sell-off on January 20, as weak earnings reactions, sustained foreign investor selling and rising global geopolitical tensions rattle investor confidence. But fund managers say much of the pressure is global in nature, and India’s markets remain relatively resilient due to their inward-looking structure and already priced-in risks, supporting continued confidence in Indian markets.

Trump. Tariffs and Earnings leave markets in the red

Indian equity markets sold off sharply on Tuesday, with the Sensex plunging over 1,000 points and the Nifty falling below 25,250, erasing nearly Rs 10 lakh crore in market capitalisation amid broad-based selling. Markets declined broadly, with mid- and small-cap stocks hit the hardest and market breadth weakening significantly. Foreign institutional investors also continued to pare exposure, extending a trend that has seen Indian equities underperform global peers over the past year. On February 20, FIIs net sold equities worth nearly Rs 3,000 crores.

Global developments have played a central role in shaping sentiment. Heightened geopolitical tensions, uncertainty around global trade and renewed focus on tariffs dominated investor discussions, amidst the ongoing World Economic Forum in Davos. Comments around trade protectionism, including continued rhetoric from US President Donald Trump on imposing higher tariffs, threats on Greenland etc., added to concerns about the global growth outlook and cross-border trade flows.

Chirag Mehta, Chief Investment Officer and Fund Manager at Quantum AMC, noted that the current volatility is not driven by domestic fundamentals. “It’s more related to global uncertainties emanating from tariff-related issues and geopolitical developments,” Mehta said. He added that this is not an India-centric issue. “India is more inward-looking, and much of the impact is already priced in,” he added.

Related Stories

  • Trading Plan: Will Nifty 50, Bank Nifty bounce back after major correction?
  • Union Budget 2026: Market expects sustained capex push, limited surprises for roads and railways
  • Trade Spotlight: How should you trade HDFC Life Insurance, Mastek, Dalmia Bharat, JK Cement, Lodha D...

Of course not all see this as a purely global driven rout. Earnings disappointment also compounded negative sentiment. Aishvarya Dadheech, Founder & Chief Investment Officer at Fident Asset Management, said the scale of Monday’s sell-off was unexpected, especially since geopolitical tensions over the weekend had not immediately impacted markets. “What actually played out is the market’s disappointment with the earnings season,” he said. Despite several large companies reporting results broadly in line with expectations, stocks saw sharp declines. “The market didn’t really appreciate the numbers,” he said, citing heavy falls in names such as ICICI Bank, HDFC Bank and Havells.

Dadheech added that selling pressure has been particularly intense in small- and micro-cap stocks, with a weak advance-decline ratio and continued foreign investor selling aggravating the correction. He also noted a lack of meaningful excitement ahead of the Union Budget. “For the first time, there is no excitement around the budget. The market is not discounting anything meaningful right now,” he said, adding that volatility is likely to remain elevated over the next few weeks.

Similarly, Alok Agarwal, Head – Quant & Fund Manager at Alchemy Capital Management, said the market reaction reflects a build-up of macro concerns alongside global risks. “The month started with poor GST numbers for December and subdued capex data for November,” he said, adding that the pressure to meet budgeted targets has increased amid slower economic momentum and weaker tax collections. “Q3 earnings have just begun, but the numbers are not too inspiring so far,” Agarwal added.

While concerns remain, Mehta noted that India is in a much better position. He highlighted that after nearly 1.5 years of relative underperformance, India’s valuation premium has narrowed. As global uncertainties subside, India should see lesser impact compared to other markets. “The impact should start reducing from here,” he said, while also noting that any positive budget measures on capital gains could further support flows.

Agarwal echoed a cautiously constructive outlook, saying that while volatility may persist in the near term, medium-term prospects are supported by policy action and improving valuations. “While there is increase in volatility in near term, the coordinated steps taken by the Government and the RBI to reactivate the economy are likely to show effect in coming months. Moreover the last 16 months of correction and record underperformance (versus EM and World Indices), have made the risk reward of Indian markets quite attractive. We are selectively positive on the markets, especially in the areas that are showing good growth prospects,” he said.

Investor concern valid, yet opportunity remains

Fund managers acknowledged that near-term volatility is a key concern for investors but said the current correction is also creating selective opportunities. Mehta stressed on the importance of a disciplined, bottom-up approach. “We were cautious on markets over the last year and kept cash levels elevated,” he said.

“As markets have corrected and many mid- and small-cap stocks are down 30–40 percent or more, there is potential opportunity,” Mehta said adding that investment decisions need to be stock-specific and factor in how global events could affect earnings.

Dadheech added that they remain bullish and do not believe in taking cash calls. Their focus remains on fundamentals rather than trying to time the market. “We are a long-only firm and focus on earnings and company fundamentals,” he said. He added that valuations across several companies have become attractive and that policy measures taken by the government and the Reserve Bank of India should begin reflecting in corporate numbers in the coming quarters. “These are the kinds of markets where investors can generate higher alpha,” he said, highlighting sectors such as autos and auto ancillaries, mid-sized banks, consumption and IT for opportunities.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: Asian shares extend selloff, global bond rout stokes fresh anxiety
data.description:
In early trade, MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan fell 0.3%. Japan's Nikkei slumped 1.2%, down for the fifth straight day.

Asian stocks extended their losses for a third session on Wednesday, undone by heightened tensions over U.S. threats to acquire Greenland ahead of President Donald Trump's Davos speech, while a global bond rout appeared to slow for now.

Fears of offshore selling of U.S. assets - the so-called "Sell America" trade that emerged after last year's "Liberation Day" tariff announcements in April - gripped markets as Wall Street tumbled over 2% overnight and the U.S. dollar suffered its biggest fall in over a month.

That sent investors fleeing to the safety of gold and silver, which both notched record highs.

"The 'sell America' trade was the driving force behind major market moves overnight, as investors looked to reduce exposure to the U.S., seen by many as an unreliable partner pursuing self-defeating policies," said Mantas Vanagas, a senior economist at Westpac.

Trump, however, doubled down on his rhetoric over Greenland, saying there was "no going back" on his goal to control the island, refusing to rule out taking it by force. His threat of tariffs on Europe has also rekindled fears of a global trade war.

The European Union will convene an emergency summit in Brussels on Thursday to discuss the matter, with the long-standing U.S.-EU alliance clearly at risk.

All eyes are now on the World Economic Forum in Davos where Trump is due to deliver a speech on Wednesday.

In early trade, MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan fell 0.3%. Japan's Nikkei slumped 1.2%, down for the fifth straight day.

Nasdaq futures and S&P 500 futures advanced 0.2% after Wall Street suffered the biggest daily drop in three months overnight. The S&P 500 lost 2.06% while the Nasdaq Composite tumbled 2.4%.

Both EURO STOXX 50 futures and DAX futures fell 0.4%.

JAPAN'S BOND ROUT PAUSES FOR NOW

The global bond market was still reeling from a brutal selloff, having been caught up in a perfect storm of worries over exposure to U.S. assets and a surge in Japanese government bond yields.

Market worries over increased government spending under Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi sent bond yields there skyrocketing to record highs.

Investors were trying to catch their breath in early trading. Providing some relief to frayed debt market nerves, the 40-year Japanese government bond yields retreated 6 basis points on Wednesday to 4.145% after surging 26 bps a day earlier to a record high of 4.215%. Liquidity in other tenors remains thin.

U.S. Treasury yields were also steady on Wednesday. The benchmark 10-year yield slipped 1 bp to 4.285%, having jumped 7 bp overnight to a five-month high of 4.313% amid the "Sell America" fears.

Danish pension fund AkademikerPension said on Tuesday it would sell off its holding of U.S. Treasuries, worth some $100 million, by the end of this month, blaming weak U.S. government finances.

In the currency markets, the U.S. dollar held steady at 98.56 against its major peers, having dropped 0.5% overnight - the biggest daily fall since early December.

The yen was steady at 158.19 per dollar, but lost out on a number of crosses, with the Swiss franc hitting a record high of 200.19 yen.

The Bank of Japan meets on Friday and though no rate hike is expected this time, policymakers could flag a tightening as soon as April.

Oil prices fell as pressure from geopolitical tensions and an expected build-up in U.S. crude inventories outweighed a temporary halt in output at two large fields in Kazakhstan.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices for March fell 1.31% to $59.57 a barrel. [O/R]

Gold prices rose 0.8% to $4,806 an ounce, a new record high, while silver climbed 0.4% to $95.01, just short of a record top of $95.87 hit on Tuesday.

data.title: Highway Infrastructure shares in focus on Rs 64.68 crore NHAI contract win
data.description:
Highway Infrastructure

H05

Highway Infrastructure share price will remain in focus following the company received an order worth Rs 64.68 crore from NHAI.

Highway Infrastructure shares will remain in focus after the company received a Letter of Award (LoA) worth Rs 64.68 crore from the National Highways Authority of India (NHAI).

In an exchange filing, the company said it has been appointed as the user-fee collection agency at the Mundka Fee Plaza, located on the strategically significant Urban Extension Road-11 (UER-11) corridor connecting Delhi and Haryana.

The Rs 64.68-crore project involves the operation and collection of user fees at the Mundka Toll Plaza, along with the upkeep and maintenance of associated facilities, including toilet blocks and consumables. The contract has an execution period of three months from the date of commencement.”

Catch all the market action on our live blog

Related Stories

  • Stocks to Watch Today: JSW Energy, HCL Tech, RBL Bank, Amagi Media Labs, Vikram Solar, CreditAccess,...
  • Fidelity Investments almost exits Aditya Birla Lifestyle, sells 1.76% stake in Aditya Birla Fashion
  • Q3 Results impact: Havells India shares drop 7% as profit misses estimates; Aarti Surfactants, Tips ...

Last week, the company received a Letter of Award (LoA) from the Indore Development Authority (IDA) for a key road development project valued at Rs 69.68 crore (excluding applicable GST), with an execution timeline of 2.5 years.

Additionally, on January 5, 2026, the company received a Letter of Award (LoA) worth Rs 32,00,77,777 from the National Highways Authority of India (NHAI) for user-fee operations at the Jawar Fee Plaza, located at km 37.100 on the Mathura–Devinagar Bypass in Uttar Pradesh

In the previous trading session, the stock closed at Rs 53.33, down Rs 1.15, or 2.11 percent. The share hit a 52-week high of Rs 134.89 on August 14, 2025, and a 52-week low of Rs 53.00 on January 20, 2026.

At the current level, the stock is trading 60.46 percent below its 52-week high and 0.62 percent above its 52-week low. The company’s market capitalisation stands at Rs 382.49 crore.

The benchmark indices were caught in a bear trap, with the Nifty 50 falling 1.38 percent on January 20. Market breadth was heavily dominated by bears, with about 2,552 shares declining against 388 rising shares on the NSE. The market may consolidate with a negative bias after decisively breaking below the previous week’s low. Below are some short-term trading ideas to consider:

Amol Athawale, VP Technical Research at Kotak Securities

Britannia Industries | CMP: Rs 5,884

After the short-term correction in Britannia from higher levels, the downward momentum has stalled and the stock has entered a consolidation phase over the last few sessions. Moreover, the stock is trading near its important demand zone.

Related Stories

  • Union Budget 2026: Market expects sustained capex push, limited surprises for roads and railways
  • Trade setup for January 21: Top 15 things to know before the opening bell

The structure suggests a revival of the uptrend from current levels in the near future. For traders, Rs 5,660 would be the key support level to watch. Above this level, the uptrend structure could continue towards Rs 6,300.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 6,300

Stop-Loss: Rs 5,660

Oil and Natural Gas Corporation | CMP: Rs 240.39

After the sharp up move over the last couple of sessions, ONGC witnessed profit booking from higher levels. However, closing near the important retracement zone and the short-term moving average suggests a bullish continuation chart structure. Therefore, the stock is likely to resume its uptrend in the coming sessions.

For the next few trading sessions, Rs 232 could be the trend-decider level for the bulls. If the stock sustains above this level, a further uptrend towards Rs 257 can be expected.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 257

Stop-Loss: Rs 232

HDFC Life Insurance Company | CMP: Rs 732.1

On the daily timeframe, HDFC Life has been in a downtrend. Therefore, it is currently in oversold territory and is trading near its demand area. The texture of the chart formation and the RSI indicate that the stock is likely to rebound for a new leg of the up move from its demand zone.

For positional traders, Rs 705 would be the decisive level. Trading above this level, the uptrend formation may continue towards Rs 785. However, if the stock closes below Rs 705, traders may prefer to exit long positions.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 785

Stop-Loss: Rs 705

Ashish Kyal, CMT, Founder and CEO of Waves Strategy Advisors

Mastek | CMP: Rs 2,223.8

On the daily chart, Mastek has been trending higher for the past one week and has outperformed broader market indices by consistently closing above the previous day’s high over the last three trading sessions, highlighting underlying strength. The stock has already confirmed a breakout from a triangle pattern on the daily chart, which is a positive technical development.

Additionally, the Bollinger Bands have begun to expand, indicating an increase in volatility. For now, a sustained move above Rs 2,245 is required for bullish momentum to continue, which could drive prices towards the prior swing high zone of Rs 2,370–2,400, as long as the Rs 2,150 level remains protected on the downside.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 2,370, Rs 2,400

Stop-Loss: Rs 2,150

Dalmia Bharat | CMP: Rs 2,191.4

Dalmia Bharat has been forming a Cup and Handle pattern since October 2025. In the previous session, the stock gained over 1.5 percent and managed to close near the neckline around the Rs 2,200 level. A sustainable breakout above this zone could pave the way for a fresh rally. The stock continues to form higher highs and higher lows, indicating positive price action.

Additionally, the consistent green histogram following a bullish crossover of the MACD provides double confirmation of the bullish outlook. For now, a decisive move above Rs 2,220 may trigger fresh buying interest, which can push prices towards Rs 2,400, the first target as per the length of the handle. On the downside, the Rs 2,110 level is expected to act as key support.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 2,400

Stop-Loss: Rs 2,110

JK Cement | CMP: Rs 5,891.5

In the previous session, JK Cement gained 1.84 percent, supported by the highest trading volumes seen since September 19, 2025. The stock has consistently held above the prior day’s low for the last five trading sessions, indicating buying from lower levels. Since November 2025, JK Cement has been trading within a rectangular range of Rs 5,361–6,030, suggesting a phase of accumulation.

In the previous session, prices attempted a breakout from this range but failed to close above it. The RSI is currently hovering near 55, indicating the absence of a strong trending move. For now, a decisive breakout above Rs 6,030 could trigger fresh upside momentum towards the Rs 6,450 level, with Rs 5,790 acting as an important support.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 6,450

Stop-Loss: Rs 5,790

Aditya Thukral, Founder & Analyst of AT Research & Risk Managers

Delhivery | CMP: Rs 384.85

Delhivery exhibited a box pattern, and a breakdown from this pattern was witnessed on January 12, accompanied by rising volumes. The stock is consistently trading below all its major exponential moving averages, namely the 10-day, 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day EMAs, all of which are sloping downwards. An established downtrend is evident, marked by the formation of lower highs and lower lows. Additionally, the stock has recorded its lowest close since July 3, 2025.

A continuation of the downtrend is expected following the breakdown from the box pattern. Any minor bounce towards the previous support level of Rs 391 will act as a selling opportunity, as prior supports have turned into resistances. Sell Delhivery Futures on a rise around Rs 391, with a stop-loss at Rs 398, targeting Rs 377 over the next two weeks.

Strategy: Sell

Target: Rs 377

Stop-Loss: Rs 398

Suzlon Energy | CMP: Rs 46.34

Suzlon entered a correction phase after forming a top around the Rs 86 level on September 12, 2024. The stock is consistently trading below all its major exponential moving averages—10-day, 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day EMAs—and has established a clear downtrend with the formation of lower highs and lower lows. Additionally, the stock has closed at its lowest level since June 6, 2024.

With prices now moving towards the targets of a bearish flag pattern on the daily chart, and the breakdown occurring with rising volumes, the pace of the downtrend is accelerating due to falling trendlines. This presents a short-selling opportunity in the futures segment.

Any minor bounce towards the previous support level of Rs 47.50 should be used as a selling opportunity, as it has now turned into resistance. Sell Suzlon Futures on a rise around Rs 47.50, with a stop-loss at Rs 48.60, targeting Rs 45.20 and Rs 43.50 over the next two weeks.

Strategy: Sell

Target: Rs 45.20, Rs 43.50

Stop-Loss: Rs 48.60

Lodha Developers | CMP: Rs 979.3

Lodha has confirmed a medium-term downtrend with the break of the swing low of Rs 1,035.15, formed during the previous corrective leg. The stock is witnessing another leg of correction after forming a top near Rs 1,530. It is consistently trading below all its major exponential moving averages—10-day, 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day EMAs—all of which are sloping downward. The short-term downtrend has now extended into a medium-term downtrend, marked by lower highs and lower lows. Additionally, the stock has recorded its lowest close since December 29, 2023.

The realty sector has remained under pressure and has been one of the biggest losers during the ongoing correction, underperforming for several months. A continuation of the downtrend is expected in this realty stock, given the established medium-term downtrend. Any minor bounce towards the previous support level of Rs 1,003 should be used as a selling opportunity, as prior supports have turned into resistances. Sell Lodha Futures on a rise around Rs 1,003, with a stop-loss at Rs 1,051, targeting Rs 926 over the next three weeks.

Strategy: Sell

Target: Rs 926

Stop-Loss: Rs 1,051

data.title: Union Budget 2026: Market expects sustained capex push, limited surprises for roads and railways
data.description:
For the roads sector, experts note that visibility remains steady but subdued, with the focus shifting away from aggressive project awards towards monetisation and selective expansion.
  • Budget to increase funding for roads and railways infrastructure
  • Focus shifts to asset monetisation, PPP formats, and selective expansion
  • Railway budget increase likely modest, safety and signalling remain key areas
Did our AI summary help?

LT

Market expects expect continuity in the government’s infrastructure-led growth strategy, with sustained capital support for roads, railways and urban infrastructure rather than headline-grabbing policy shifts. Analysts believe the Budget will reinforce long-term priorities such as logistics efficiency, multimodal connectivity and capacity augmentation, even as fiscal consolidation remains in focus.

Key Trends

· Continued emphasis on infrastructure as a growth engine

· Roads and railways remain central to logistics and connectivity goals

· Capacity expansion and decongestion take precedence over new reforms

Related Stories

  • Trade Spotlight: How should you trade HDFC Life Insurance, Mastek, Dalmia Bharat, JK Cement, Lodha D...
  • Trade setup for January 21: Top 15 things to know before the opening bell

· Increasing focus on asset monetisation and private participation

Key Challenges

· Balancing higher capital outlay with fiscal discipline

· Execution risks and land acquisition delays

· Monetisation frameworks still evolving

· Funding requirements for large, long-gestation projects

Last Budget Highlights

· Capital outlay of Rs. 2.65 lakh crore for Railways

· Continued push for highway expansion under MoRTH

· Focus on Gati Shakti Master Plan and multimodal logistics

· Support for asset monetisation

· Higher allocations for urban infrastructure and connectivity

Industry Expectations This Budget

· Increase in allocation for the Ministry of Road Transport & Highways

· Higher spending on highway expansion and faster clearances

· Increased focus on rail capacity, safety and modernisation

· Support for urban infrastructure—sanitation, mobility and water

· Greater use of monetisation frameworks to attract private capital

According to Axis Securities, capital outlay for the infrastructure sector is expected to witness a modest-to-high increase in the Union Budget 2026–27, reflecting the government’s continued emphasis on infrastructure as a growth engine. The brokerage expects a 9–10% year-on-year increase in allocations for the Ministry of Road Transport & Highways, while railways could see a sharper rise of 10-15% driven by capacity expansion and safety requirements.

However, rating agency ICRA expects a more measured trajectory for railway spending. “ICRA anticipates a range-bound increase in Railways’ budgetary allocation for FY2026–27, given the trend seen over the past two years (around 5% YoY growth in FY2025 and FY2026BE to reach Rs. 2.65 lakh crore, including EBR of Rs. 10,000 crore),” said Suprio Banerjee, Vice President & Co-Group Head, ICRA.

Sector Outlook

For the roads sector, experts note that visibility remains steady but subdued, with the focus shifting away from aggressive project awards towards monetisation and selective expansion. NHAI has slowed new awards as it reworks concession structures and pivots towards PPP formats from EPC and hybrid annuity models. Interest in PPP assets remains limited due to high capital intensity, resulting in a smaller bidder pool and slower awarding activity. As a result, asset monetisation—through toll-operate-transfer projects and InvITs—has emerged as a clearer priority than greenfield construction.

Shah reiterates that monetisation could be the key differentiator. “There is a clear intent to monetise existing assets such as airports, ports and roads that are already part of the pipeline,” she says. Incremental project announcements are now more visible at the state level, particularly for expressways and regional connectivity, rather than through NHAI.

Valuations in the roads space remain benign due to muted order-flow visibility. However, diversified infrastructure and EPC players with exposure across segments and geographies have continued to see relatively better project inflows, while single-sector-focused companies have lagged. Despite the absence of near-term triggers, diversified players remain preferred due to superior earnings visibility.

For railways, expectations remain cautious amid the government’s continued emphasis on fiscal discipline. Market participants expect any increase in railway allocations to be modest, with focus shifting from headline numbers to specific announcements. Elara’s Harshit Kapadia says the street is bracing for a limited increase this year. “The expectation on the street is that any increase in the railway budget is likely to be capped at around five percent,” he notes.

According to Kapadia, markets will track announcements around rolling stock, locomotive orders, metro projects and signalling. “Railway stocks are extremely sensitive to budget announcements because railway CAPEX directly drives order inflows,” he says. A flat or modest increase could keep stocks range-bound, while “a meaningful increase, particularly in double digits, would likely trigger a sharp positive reaction,” especially after last year’s correction.

With electrification largely complete, budgetary focus is expected to shift towards decongestion through new lines, track doubling, gauge conversion and dedicated freight corridors. Safety and signalling remain key monitorables, with developments related to the Kavach system keeping signalling and systems stocks in focus around the Budget.

Stocks to Watch

Diversified infrastructure players such as L&T, Ashoka Buildcon, HG Infra and NCC in roads and in railways, PSU names are likely to stay in focus around the Budget, with RITES remaining a preferred play, while Titagarh Rail Systems stands out among non-PSU manufacturers. Within railway-linked ancillaries, stocks exposed to signalling and safety systems such as CG Power, HBL Power and Kernex Microsystems could see incremental interest depending on announcements related to rolling stock, signalling and the Kavach safety framework.

data.title: Trading Plan: Will Nifty 50, Bank Nifty bounce back after major correction?
data.description:
Nifty Trading Plan for January 21
  • Nifty 50 may attempt a bounce given RSI reaches the oversold zone
  • On higher side, 25,300–25,450 zone will be crucial to monitor
  • Fall below 200-day EMA could further strengthen bearish control
  • Bank Nifty likely to consolidate
Did our AI summary help?

After a severe correction that pulled the Nifty 50 down to a three-month low, as bears tightened their grip on the market, the index may attempt a bounce given that the RSI has reached the oversold zone. However, sustainability remains the key factor to watch amid prevailing bearish sentiment. In the event of a bounce, the 25,300–25,450 zone will be crucial to monitor. However, a fall below the 200-day EMA could further strengthen bearish control. Meanwhile, the Bank Nifty is likely to consolidate. Support is placed at 59,000, followed by 58,800–58,700 as a crucial support zone. In case of a rebound, the index may face resistance at 59,600, followed by 60,000 as a key hurdle, experts said.

On January 20, the Nifty 50 plunged 353 points (1.38 percent) to 25,233, while the Bank Nifty dropped 487 points (0.81 percent) to 59,404, accompanied by further weakness in market breadth. A total of 2,552 shares declined, compared to 388 shares that advanced on the NSE.

Nifty Outlook and Strategy

Ashish Kyal, CMT, Founder and CEO of Waves Strategy Advisors

Over the past 10 trading sessions, Nifty has failed to close above the prior day’s high, reflecting sustained selling pressure. After breaking down from a wedge pattern on the daily chart, the index has declined nearly 700 points, driven by mixed corporate earnings, persistent FII selling, and rising concerns over US tariffs imposed by Donald Trump. Nifty has retraced 23.6 percent of the entire rise from the lows of April 2025.

Related Stories

  • Union Budget 2026: Market expects sustained capex push, limited surprises for roads and railways
  • Trade Spotlight: How should you trade HDFC Life Insurance, Mastek, Dalmia Bharat, JK Cement, Lodha D...

On the daily chart, Nifty has been closing below its 100-day EMA (Exponential Moving Average) for the past two sessions for the first time since September 2025, which is a negative signal. However, momentum indicators are near oversold levels, suggesting that the possibility of minor pullbacks cannot be ruled out. One can use pullbacks to sell, with the next key support lying near the 25,060 level.

The overall trend for Nifty remains on the side of bears. One may adopt a sell-on-rise approach instead of attempting to catch bottoms, with targets of 25,060 or lower. On the upside, a break above 25,620, which coincides with the 100-day EMA, is required for any meaningful reversal.

Key Resistance: 25,620

Key Support: 25,060

Strategy: Short positions can be created if Nifty Futures move towards 25,350 and reverse on the 5-minute chart, with a stop-loss at 25,500 and a target of 25,060.

Aditya Thukral, Founder & Analyst of AT Research & Risk Managers

The downtrend continuation in the Nifty 50 emerged after the break of the swing pivot at 25,473. The slide continued after the breakdown of a minor consolidation between 25,432 and 25,500, and the index plunged to the low of 25,171.35, approaching the 200-day EMA, which is currently placed around 25,160. As the index is now close to the 200-day EMA, it may remain within a broader range of 25,000–25,480 for the next couple of days.

Technically, the downtrend continuation remains intact, as another leg of decline began after a four-day consolidation that formed a box pattern between 25,473 and 25,900. The breakdown of this 427-point box pattern suggests that the index is likely to test 25,050 soon. At the same time, the 14-period RSI is in the oversold zone on both daily and shorter timeframes. Minor bounces may emerge, but these are likely to provide selling opportunities. This selling pressure will ease only if the index closes decisively above 25,500.

Key Resistance: 25,450, 25,600

Key Support: 25,050, 24,800

Strategy: Sell Nifty Futures (January contract) on a rise around 25,340, with a stop-loss at 25,541, targeting 25,090.

Preeti K Chabra, Founder of Trade Delta

On the daily timeframe, Nifty formed a strong bearish candle and continues to trade within a steep downward-sloping channel, where any rebound is met with fresh selling pressure, clearly reinforcing bearish control. The index is trading below the 40-day EMA at 25,864 and the 20-day SMA (middle Bollinger Band) at 25,942, indicating sustained selling pressure at higher levels.

On the downside, 24,969 emerges as a crucial support level, coinciding with the 78.6 percent Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the September 30 low of 24,587 to the January 5 high of 26,373. A decisive breakdown below this level could accelerate further weakness. On the upside, the 50 percent retracement level at 25,480 is acting as immediate resistance. The daily RSI at 29.2, positioned well below its signal line, reflects strong bearish momentum and highlights the absence of buying strength.

On the weekly timeframe, Nifty is currently taking support near the 40-week EMA at 25,195. A sustained breach below this critical support could resume medium-term selling pressure and deepen the corrective phase.

From a derivatives perspective, weekly options data continues to signal a bearish undertone, with unwinding observed in in-the-money Put options. Overall, the broader structure remains bearish, and unless Nifty decisively reclaims its key moving averages, the index is likely to remain under pressure with a downside bias.

Key Resistance: 25,480, 25,585

Key Support: 25,195, 24,969

Strategy: Consider selling Nifty Futures near 25,480, targeting 25,195, followed by 24,969, with a stop-loss at 25,585.

Bank Nifty - Outlook and Positioning

Ashish Kyal, CMT, Founder and CEO of Waves Strategy Advisors

At the start of the week, Bank Nifty faced resistance near the 60,200 mark and reversed on the downside. Private banks were the key contributors to the decline.

On the daily chart, Bank Nifty has closed below the middle Bollinger Band after reversing from the upper band, signaling a potential move towards the lower band, which is placed near 58,900. The index has retraced 61.8 percent of the recent rise that began on January 12, while the next 78.6 percent Fibonacci support lies near 59,190.

Overall sentiment remains weak. Pullbacks can be used as shorting opportunities for a move towards 59,190, followed by 58,900. On the upside, a break above 60,200 is required for any meaningful reversal.

Key Resistance: 60,200

Key Support: 58,900

Strategy: Short positions can be initiated if Bank Nifty Futures move towards 59,600 and reverse on the 15-minute chart, with a stop-loss at 59,800 and a target of 59,190.

Aditya Thukral, Founder & Analyst of AT Research & Risk Managers

The short-term trend of the banking index appears sideways, as the index is hovering between 58,700 and 60,400. However, the previous upmove from 58,864 to 60,235 appears corrective in nature, and a test of lower support levels around 58,700–58,900 seems possible. Considering that prices have now closed below the 20-day EMA, which is sloping downward, the short-term trend has turned negative.

From the derivatives segment, aggressive long unwinding and short buildup suggest anticipation of further downside risk in the index, with limited upside potential. This has turned market sentiment negative.

Key Resistance: 59,600, 60,000

Key Support: 59,100, 58,700

Strategy: Sell Bank Nifty Futures (January contract) on a rise around 59,700, with a stop-loss above 60,000, targeting 59,200.

Preeti K Chabra, Founder of Trade Delta

Bank Nifty formed a strong bearish candle on the daily chart and closed below the 20-day SMA (middle Bollinger Band) at 59,571, reinforcing bearish momentum. However, it managed to close above the 40-day EMA at 59,249 and the 38.2 percent Fibonacci retracement level at 59,184, calculated from the rally between the November 7 low of 57,157 and the January 5 high of 60,437, thereby reinforcing a strong near-term support base. A decisive breakdown below this level could accelerate further correction.

Momentum indicators continue to support a negative bias. The daily RSI at 48.8 is trading below its signal line, indicating a gradual buildup of bearish momentum.

From a derivatives perspective, monthly options data remains bearish, as unwinding in in-the-money put options signals a negative outlook. Overall, Bank Nifty is showing signs of weakness, and traders may consider adopting a sell-on-rise strategy as long as the index remains below the resistance zone.

Key Resistance: 59,571, 59,800

Key Support: 59,184, 58,800

Strategy: Sell Bank Nifty Futures near the cash reference level of 59,571, targeting 59,184, followed by 58,800, while maintaining a stop-loss at 59,800.

data.title: Arohan Financial Services plans to hit capital market during middle of 2026-27 with Rs 1,500-crore IPO
data.description:
Arohan Financial Services IPO News

NBFC-MFI Arohan Financial Services plans to hit the capital market during the middle of next financial year with an IPO of Rs 1,500 crore, managing director Manoj Kumar Nambiar said.

The company has already appointed merchant bankers and hopes to file the draft red herring prospectus (DRHP) with SEBI in the next two months, he said.

"We plan to hit the capital market to raise Rs 1,500 crore through an IPO in the second quarter of next financial year. Out of the total amount, Rs 750 crore would be raised by issue of fresh shares and Rs 750 crore as offer for sale (OFS) by old investors like Tano Capital and Michael and Susan Dell Foundation", Nambiar told PTI.

Click Here To Read All IPO News

Nambiar said there will be no stake sale by the promoters.

Related Stories

  • EAAA India Alternatives files draft papers with SEBI for Rs 1,500-crore IPO
  • Aritas Vinyl IPO sees over 2 times subscription on final day, Digilogic Systems offer booked 31% on ...
  • Amagi Media Labs shares likely to make tepid listing on Wednesday; here's a trading strategy

He said that the MFI sector is seeing a positive momentum in the last three to four months, with collection efficiency ratio (CER) increasing following a good harvest and GST rates rationalisation. CER of Arohan stands at 99.7 percent. Nambiar said Arohan is using AI tools for collections and recoveries.

According to him, the IPO will help strengthen the capital base of the MFI and build the foundation of an efficient management and credible board governance.

Nambiar said that it is expected that the government will come out with a credit guarantee scheme, following which banks will feel more comfortable to give credit to the MFI sector.

"There are some issues which are needed to be resolved for the betterment of the sector. The credit bureaus need to update data on borrowers on quicker intervals, which in turn will help in lending", he said.

Nambiar said that the MFI industry has seen a dip in total outstanding and the number of borrowers, adding that this trend will be reversed in the coming quarters.

By the end of the current financial year, Arohan is eyeing an outstanding portfolio of Rs 7,000 crore, which is targeted to touch Rs 20,000 crore by 2030. Its network stands at Rs 2,100 crore, he said.

Arohan plans to diversify into secured products like gold loans and loans against property.

data.title: First Tick: Top global cues to watch in today’s trade
data.description:
Nifty Trading Plan for January 21

Indian benchmark indices, Sensex and Nifty, are likely see a flat to positive start on January 21, with cues coming from GIFT Nifty, which was trading higher at around 25,294.

Track the latest updates on GIFT Nifty right here on Moneycontrol.

Dalal Street witnessed a sharp sell-off on January 20, with benchmark indices plunging more than 1 percent each, dragging the Nifty below the 25,200 mark, intraday, amid concerns over global trade tensions, mixed quarterly earnings, weak global cues, a falling rupee, broad-based selling, and continued FII outflows.

At close, the Sensex was down 1065.78 points or 1.28 percent at 82,180.47, and the Nifty was down 353 points or 1.38 percent at 25,232.50.

Here is how financial markets across the globe fared overnight:

GIFT Nifty (Gains)

Related Stories

  • Trading Plan: Will Nifty 50, Bank Nifty bounce back after major correction?
  • Trade setup for January 21: Top 15 things to know before the opening bell
  • Will the bloodbath on D-Street continue or can a short-term relief be expected? Market experts say t...

The GIFT Nifty is trading higher at around 25,294, indicating a flat to positive start for the day.

Asian Equities (Slip)

Asian stocks retreated on Wednesday, tracking a selloff on Wall Street as the US-Europe trade conflict over Greenland and turmoil in bond markets rattled investors.

US Equities (Down)

All ​three major Wall Street indexes ended Tuesday with their biggest one-day drops in three months, in a broad selloff triggered by concerns ‌that fresh tariff threats from President Donald Trump against Europe could signal renewed market volatility.

All three U.S. equity benchmarks registered their worst one-day performance since October 10, with both the S&P 500 (.SPX), opens new tab and Nasdaq Composite (.IXIC), opens new tab slipping below their 50-day moving averages.

The S&P 500 lost 143.15 points, or 2.06%, to ‌end at 6,796.86 points, while the Nasdaq Composite gave up 561.07 points, or 2.39%, to 22,954.32. The Dow Jones Industrial ​Average fell 870.74 points, or 1.76%, to 48,488.59.

US Bond Yield (Up)

Treasuries joined a selloff in global bonds on Tuesday as escalating trade tensions, spurred by proposed US tariffs relating to Greenland, undermined demand for American assets.

The yield curve bear steepened as cash trading resumed following a US holiday, with 10- and 30-year yields both climbing three basis points in early Asia trading.

Dollar Index (Down)

The dollar fell as President Donald Trump continues pushing with his plan on Greenland, sparking risks of a bigger trade rift with Europe.

Asian Currencies (Up)

Asian Currencies were trading mostly higher in the early trade on Wednesday, with the Thai Baht leading the gainers, followed by Philippines Peso, the South Korean Won, Japanese Won.

Crude (Slips)

West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices fell on Wednesday as pressure from geopolitical tensions and an expected build-up in U.S. crude inventories outweighed a temporary halt in output at two large fields in Kazakhstan.

Gold (Rises)

Gold rose to a record high and silver was near an all-time peak as the worsening crisis over Greenland and a meltdown in Japanese government debt supported haven demand.

Fund Flow Action

Foreign institutional investors (FIIs) continued their selling on January 20, offloading equities worth around Rs 2,938 crore, while domestic institutional investors (DIIs) bought shares worth approximately Rs 3,665 crore.

Hope you're all set for today's trade. We wish you a profitable day ahead.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.
